issue_title: [QUESTION] Experiment with alternative task scheduling policies
issue_body:
IMHO, the `curio` library actually looks more like an operating system, rather than just a ***library for performing concurrent I/O***, which I think is intentional. Therefore many aspects of the OS development also apply to it. We already have a `Kernel`, a context of execution (aka **Task-local storage**), processes (aka ***Tasks***), etc. 

So, one thing that I notice: the scheduling policy is a FIFO policy (this is actually the case for all major asynchronous libraries and frameworks), which is the simplest one, but by far not the optimal one. 

Given that `curio.Task` already has some execution stats (`Task.cycles`, `Task.state`, `Task. _last_io `), we can use this stats to come up with more sophisticated scheduling, like *Multilevel Feedback Queue*, which should improve the performance and usability under the real world workload. There were some experiments with threads scheduling already done: http://dabeaz.blogspot.com.au/2010_02_01_archive.html, which demonstrated a performance boost in a threaded environment, which should also apply to the coroutines world as well.

So the question is: should we try to use an advanced scheduling in `curio` and what benefits if any it'd bring?

- Go to zendesk and add a custom field in your ticket (admin > ticket fields > add custom field)
- Add a custom field and copy-paste the ID
- If you now go to the ticket view you will be able to see new fields on the left-hand side of the ticket.

You can now fill this custom field at Ticket creation by doing this API call:

```bash
curl https://[SUBDOMAIN].zendesk.com/api/v2/tickets.json -d '{"ticket": { "custom_fields": [{"id": [CUSTOM FIELD ID], "value": "HELLO"}], "requester": {"name": "John Doe", "email": "john@domain.com"}, "subject": "This is the subject!", "comment": { "body": "This is the question." }}}' -H "Content-Type: application/json" -v -u [EMAIL ADDRESS]/token:[TOKEN] -X POST
```

It would be great if zenpy supported this, perhaps as:

```python
Ticket(custom_fields=[CustomField(id=1234, value="Hello", CustomField(id=1235, value=15), CustomField(id=1236, value=datetime.now()], ...)
```

Custom fields can be strings, numbers or dates.

It does seem that it takes a while for custom fields to become "active" in the API. I.e. there is some delay between creating a custom field and actually being able to POST it via the API...

issue_title: Document that update works differently to normally dictionary
issue_body:
Looking at the source of addict, I noticed that the `update` method helpfully recurses into nested dictionary to do the update. That isn't documented though, and could be a surprise since it's inconsistent with the base `dict` class. For example:
```python
>>> d = {'a': {'b': 3}}
>>> d.update({'a': {'c': 4}})
>>> print(d)
{'a': {'c': 4}}
>>> D = Dict({'a': {'b': 3}})

issue_title: Use tox for tests in travis
issue_body: Currently fusesoc runs tests in travis with "pip install .". This prevents catching bugs which happen due to wrong packaging, i.e. when installing from sdist. Symptom connection.char_write_handle() takes a very long time process with a lot of the time spent waiting for lock. I have just been checking out yas3fs as I thought it might be useful in my application.

FYI: S3 and EC2 where I ran my tests are both in the same region.

The first test I did was to mount one of my buckets and then attempt to do an operation (sha1 in this case) on that file. The file was ~850MB in size and I was expecting there to be a delay on the first run because the file would need to be downloaded. That turned out to be the case, but wildly more than I was expecting.

```
$ time sha1sum 03302014-r1.nd.ome.tif
efebdfcd4241fb53fc8f04d31897970db21bdca1  03302014-r1.nd.ome.tif

real	2m9.312s
user	0m2.448s
sys	0m1.318s
```

I thought that was rather a long time as I can download this file from s3 to this EC2 instance in ~15s usually. Anyway, I thought I'd then repeat the experiment to see how it performed once the file has been cached locally. I was surprised that the result was not a lot better which I guess points to the overhead of yas3fs/FUSE as the problem.

```
$ time sha1sum 03302014-r1.nd.ome.tif
efebdfcd4241fb53fc8f04d31897970db21bdca1  03302014-r1.nd.ome.tif

real	1m40.551s
user	0m2.568s
sys	0m1.245s
```

Just in case something weird was happening in the case of calculating the sha1, I also tried a basic copy operation (to a non s3 location):

```
$ time cp 03302014-r1.nd.ome.tif ~/

real	1m37.469s
user	0m0.002s
sys	0m1.280s
```

Also for reference, this is how long it takes to calculate the sha1 from the cached file:

```
$ time sha1sum /tmp/yas3fs/dpwr/files/s3test/03302014-r1.nd.ome.tif
efebdfcd4241fb53fc8f04d31897970db21bdca1  /tmp/yas3fs/dpwr/files/s3test/03302014-r1.nd.ome.tif

real	0m2.890s
user	0m2.793s
sys	0m0.099s
```

Is this likely to be a bug or is that the expected performance of FUSE and/or S3 based filesystems? I don't have any previous experience with either and I'm trying to figure out the best avenue to explore for my application.

Thanks a lot,

Douglas

Hi, 

While I like the concept of a this pattern, I feel like your examples are not reflecting its usefulness.

First, you introduce:

    def get_user_name():
        current_user = request.user
        if current_user:
            return current_user.name

        return ''

But this is not idiomatic Python code, and any mid experienced dev would write it:

    def get_user_name():
        return getattr(request.user, 'name', '')

Hence, it does not highlight any benefits of the maybe() version:

    def get_user_name():
        return maybe(request.user).name.or_else('')

Then you have this second example:

    stores = json.get('stores')
    if stores:
        products = stores[0].get('products')
        if products:
            product_name = products[0].get('details', {}).get('name') or 'unknown'

And you replace it with:

    product_name = maybe(stores)[0]['products'][0]['details']['name'].or_else('unknown')

I think this is misleading, as you remove the json.get() call you used in the first example and ignore the fact you would need to import maybe(). In reality, your exemple would read:

    from pymaybe import maybe
    stores = json.get('stores')
    product_name = maybe(stores)[0]['products'][0]['details']['name'].or_else('unknown')

But then again, this would be against PEP8 because the line is 85 characters, which is too long. So a Python dev would write it this way:

    from pymaybe import maybe
    stores = json.get('stores')
    product_name = maybe(stores)[0]['products'][0]['details']['name']
    product_name = product_name.or_else('unknown')

Additionally, your first version is not how a Python dev would write it, he or she would most likely do:

    try:
        product_name = json.get('stores')[0]['products'][0]['details']['name']
    except (TypeError, KeyError, IndeError):
        product_name = 'unknown'

Again, this does not highlight the benefits of maybe(), and even worst, the maybe() version is actually more limited.

For once, it does not honor \_\_getitem__:

        (test) >>> class Test:
            def __getitem__(self, value):
                return 1
              ...:     

        (test) >>> maybe(Test())[0]
        ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
        AttributeError                            Traceback (most recent call last)
        <ipython-input-6-e4a755e77e5b> in <module>()
        ----> 1 maybe(Test())[0]

        /home/sam/.local/share/virtualenvs/test/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pymaybe/__init__.pyc in __getitem__(self, key)
            212 
            213     def __getitem__(self, key):
        --> 214         return maybe(self.__value.get(key, None))
            215 
            216     def __setitem__(self, key, value):

        AttributeError: Test instance has no attribute 'get'

        (test) >>> Test()[0]
                   1

And it doesn't play well with advanced collections such as defaultdict:

        (test) >>> maybe(defaultdict(datetime.utcnow))[0]
                   None

        (test) >>> defaultdict(datetime.utcnow)[0]
                   datetime.datetime(2015, 7, 30, 19, 39, 28, 298488)

I'm only saying this because I actually find the concept very interesting, and I'm looking actively for a useful usecase.

issue_title: OSX install broken when using virtualenvs?
issue_body:
This lib seemed way interesting and got a bit of publicity a few weeks ago, so I decided to check it out the usual way: create a virtualenv, pip install the library inside it and play around.

Except I didn't get anywhere: `get_password` would always return `None` (with no keychain prompt) and `set_password` would throw `OSError: Can't store password in Keychain`, in both cases whatever the service & account are. After a few reinstallations, I tried compiling from scratch (cloning from mercurial and `python setup.py build`ŌĆ” success. Both tip (4a551eda6bf8) and tag 0.2 build and work perfectly.

Try to `python setup.py install` it in my virtualenv, failure again (except it now throws `keyring.backend.PasswordSetError` on `set_password`, due to being tip I guess).

Tested the same scenario in a fresh OpenSUSE image, no issue.

I wrote a script to parse php files, the first file works well, but when parsing the second php file, it raise a SyntaxError. I noticed the first file not enclosed by `?>`, I added `?>` at the file end and then the script worked.

Error:
```  File "<string>", line 855
    <
SyntaxError: invalid syntax
```

The first php file: https://github.com/typecho/typecho/blob/master/var/Typecho/Request.php
The second php file: Any code starts with `<?php`

```python
import os
from phply.phplex import lexer
from phply.phpparse import make_parser


def export(items):
    result = []
    if items:
        for item in items:
            if hasattr(item, 'generic'):
                item = item.generic(with_lineno=True)
            result.append(item)

    return result


def main():
    files = ['Request.php', 'test1.php', 'test2.php']
    for i in files:
        filename = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(__file__), i)
        if not os.path.exists(filename):
            return {}

        with open(filename) as f:
            code = f.read()

        export(make_parser().parse(code, lexer=lexer, tracking=True))


main()
```

Reification is a constrained graph transformation that replaces an edge with a binary node. For example, `A --> B` becomes `A <-- C --> B`. This allows cycles to be broken and relations to become focused (e.g., as root nodes). Not any edge can be reified, and the new node and new edges are not all the same.

* See if there's a more complete table than the one at https://github.com/amrisi/amr-guidelines/blob/master/amr.md#reification
* Create a machine-readable (CSV, JSON, etc.) version of the table (if one doesn't already exist)
* Create methods for, e.g., `reify()` and the inverse (`dereify()`? `deify()`?)

Hopefully reification can be done on a Graph without knowledge of the codec.

issue_title: re.LOCALE with str pattern deprecated on python 3.5
issue_body:
Importing mohawk with warnings turned into exceptions exposes a deprecation warning:

```
tests/test_upload.py:5: in <module>
    from mohawk import Sender
venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/mohawk/__init__.py:1: in <module>
    from .sender import *
venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/mohawk/sender.py:3: in <module>
    from .base import default_ts_skew_in_seconds, HawkAuthority, Resource
venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/mohawk/base.py:12: in <module>
    from .util import (calculate_mac,
venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/mohawk/util.py:228: in <module>
    re.LOCALE)
venv/lib/python3.5/re.py:224: in compile
    return _compile(pattern, flags)
venv/lib/python3.5/re.py:293: in _compile
    p = sre_compile.compile(pattern, flags)
venv/lib/python3.5/sre_compile.py:536: in compile
    p = sre_parse.parse(p, flags)
venv/lib/python3.5/sre_parse.py:830: in parse
    p.pattern.flags = fix_flags(str, p.pattern.flags)
venv/lib/python3.5/sre_parse.py:804: in fix_flags
    DeprecationWarning, stacklevel=6)
E   DeprecationWarning: LOCALE flag with a str pattern is deprecated. Will be an error in 3.6
```

I don't see why header verification should be dependent on the locale, I'm assuming the `\w` can be replaced by a `a-zA-Z0-9` pattern to avoid any ambiguity, and the re.LOCALE flag removed?

issue_title: Drop support for EOL Python 2.6 and 3.3?
issue_body:
It looks like significant effort is going into maintaining support for EOL Python versions. When they reach EOL that means they're no longer receiving security updates.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1324225/34263159-38b766bc-e677-11e7-918b-54bc889cfff0.png)

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CPython#Version_history


Here's the pip installs for jedi from PyPI for the last month (via `pypinfo --percent --pip --markdown jedi pyversion`).

| python_version | percent | download_count |
| -------------- | ------: | -------------: |
| 3.6            |   44.4% |        155,231 |
| 3.5            |   27.5% |         96,124 |
| 3.4            |   22.2% |         77,788 |
| 2.7            |    5.2% |         18,142 |
| 3.7            |    0.4% |          1,559 |
| 3.3            |    0.2% |            800 |
| 2.6            |    0.0% |             30 |
| 3.2            |    0.0% |              5 |


From these numbers, it looks like 2.6 and 3.3 can be safely dropped.

This would allow the code to be modernised and ease the maintenance burden quite a bit.

Thoughts?

issue_title: BSD: disk_partitions(all=False) always return an empty list
issue_body: On BSD systems there is no difference between `df` and `df -a`. The same partitions are always returned. As such psutil should ignore the `all` argument passed to `disk_partitions` as there simply is no way to distinguish between physical partitions and "others". We'll just always return them all.

issue_body:
Not sure if this is actually the case as it's something which is difficult to reproduce, but subprocess.Popen defines a `__del__` special method which free resources.
It looks like `psutil.Popen` should do the same and call the original `__del__` method.

issue_title: Add option to xso.Child descriptor for strict type checking and use it for PubSub Items and IQs
issue_body:
It is annoying that currently, if you simply forget to designate your class as payload for IQ stanzas or PubSub Items, you are able to *send* data, but from the application perspective you only get an exception (IQ stanzas) or empty item (PubSub) back.

It would be better if that would fail early, e.g. at assignment time to that descriptor. We should make the typechecking optional, because ``isinstance`` is rather expensive and in most of the cases, XSOs are an implementation detail. This is obviously different for IQ stanzas and PubSub Items, where a friendlier (in the sense of failing loud and early when you make a mistake) interface is appropriate.

Adding a ``strict`` flag to the xso.Child descriptor or even better building on top of the validator or typing interface seems reasonable.

This problem was first issued under `pipenv` repo ([pipenv/issues/1575](https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/issues/1575)) but as it turned out it was not `pipenv` that was at fault.

##### Problem description:
If we place `pywin32` (or `pypiwin32`) in `Pipfile` for example like this:
```
[packages]
"pywin32" = {version = "*", sys_platform = "== 'win32'"}
```
and then we do `pipenv install` on **Windows** everything will be fine. But if we do the same under **Linux** then we are in bad luck. Even though pipenv is not installing this package (because we specified to install it only under windows) we will still get an error:
```
Warning: Your dependencies could not be resolved. You likely have a mismatch in your sub-dependencies.
  You can use $ pipenv install --skip-lock to bypass this mechanism, then run $ pipenv graph to inspect the situation.
Could not find a version that matches pywin32>=223
Tried: (no version found at all)
```

For the matter other system-specific packages like `pywinusb` or `stdeb` work fine with pipenv in identical scenario.

As stated by `pipenv` contributors:
> (...) this is not an issue with pipenv, it's an issue with how pywin32 has organized their setup.py, we literally have no way to work around this and it is only a problem for this specific package.

issue_title: Patching a __init__ doesn't work on Py3
issue_body:
It fails with
  File "/opt/yplan/venv3/lib/python3.5/site-packages/patchy/api.py", line 174, in _set_source
    real_func.__code__ = new_code
ValueError: __init__() requires a code object with 1 free vars, not 0

From reading here:
http://stackoverflow.com/questions/4885168/how-to-make-super-work-by-manually-filling-the-class-cell

My general understanding is that to make super() work on Py3 they turned all __init__'s into closures which source __class__ from the containing scope (regardless of whether the __init__ actually uses super).
Patchy doesn't currently support closures (in general how would you access them to patch them) and so doesn't preserve the co_freevars on the code object.

issue_title: Unable to login to other instances

issue_body:
Currently _Contig is defined as

    _Contig = collections.namedtuple('Contig', ['id', 'length'])

There are other common k:v pairs in the ##contig lines. For example, the VCF 4.1 specs give the following examples (in addition to *id* and *length*):

- assembly
- md5
- species
- taxonomy
- URL

The ##contig metadata lines seem fundamentally different than the other explicitly defined metadata lines, in that the k:v pairs are left open-ended (whereas they are defined strictly for e.g. FILTER and FORMAT lines.

At a minimum, it would be nice to add these additional fields to the namedtuple. On the other hand, should contig lines be treated differently (and perhaps just as raw metadata?) given that they could conceivably contain any key/value pair?

issue_title: Scan command failed: [' * Certificate Information:', 'Unhandled exception while running --certinfo:', "TypeError - object of type 'UnrecognizedExtension' has no len()"]
issue_body: Is this a known bug?

In OpenSSL 1.1.0, support for SSL 2.0 and export cipher suites was removed. This is good for the Internet but bad for SSLyze. We also cannot stay stuck on 1.0.2 forever as new cipher suites (ChaCha20 and Poly1305) were added 1.1.0. This might require writing a plugin to check for cipher suites and protocols that are not supported by OpenSSL.

Hi!
I've a small bug issue in the Virtual Keyboard when I'm trying to remove the characters in a textarea by clicking in the BackSpace button (Bksp). If I have a text written with "tt", for example, the first "t" is not removed. This only happens using chrome. In IE11 for example, works fine!

// add combo back

Another strange behavior that I found out in this last version is that the usePreview in a TextArea is not working fine. It does not take the initial text to the preview TextArea. It's blank! Thank you so much for your support.

Best regards,
Tiago

issue_title: Query with Application Resource Locks Terminated during Processing
issue_body:
### Environment

- Python: 3.6.1 (32-bit), 3.4.3
- pyodbc: 4.0.17, 4.0.16
- OS: Windows, Linux
- DB: SQL Server 2012, SQL Server 2016
- driver: ODBC Driver 13 for SQL Server, ODBC Driver 11 for SQL Server, SQL Server Native Client 11.0

### Issue

I have a stored procedure that takes in a list of identifiers and then deletes those identifiers' data from a series of about thirty tables. In my context, it's very possible that multiple copies of this stored procedure could be running concurrently (working on different identifiers), or that other stored procedures writing to those same thirty tables will be running at the same time (again, with different identifiers). In order to avoid deadlocks from table-level locks taken out by the delete statements and the other procedures trying to run, I wrapped every delete statement in this stored procedure in an application resource lock. It looks something like the below.

```SQL
CREATE PROCEDURE [dbo].[deleteData] @IDs dbo.IDTableType READONLY
AS
BEGIN

DECLARE @result INT;

EXEC @result = sp_getapplock @Resource = 'MyTable1', @LockOwner = 'Session', @LockMode = 'Exclusive';
DELETE FROM mydb.dbo.MyTable1 WHERE ID in (SELECT ID FROM @IDS);
EXEC @result = sp_releaseapplock @Resource = 'MyTable1', @LockOwner = 'Session';

EXEC @result = sp_getapplock @Resource = 'MyTable2', @LockOwner = 'Session', @LockMode = 'Exclusive';
DELETE FROM mydb.dbo.MyTable2 WHERE ID in (SELECT ID FROM @IDS);
EXEC @result = sp_releaseapplock @Resource = 'MyTable2', @LockOwner = 'Session';

--... (etc) ...

EXEC @result = sp_getapplock @Resource = 'MyTable30', @LockOwner = 'Session', @LockMode = 'Exclusive';
DELETE FROM mydb.dbo.MyTable30 WHERE ID in (SELECT ID FROM @IDS);
EXEC @result = sp_releaseapplock @Resource = 'MyTable30', @LockOwner = 'Session';
END
```
I execute the stored procedure through something like the following python script:

```python
import pyodbc

connection_string = 'Driver=ODBC Driver 13 for SQL Server;ApplicationIntent=ReadWrite;MultiSubnetFailover=No;' \
                            'Trusted_Connection=yes;Server=localhost;Database=mydb'
db_handle = pyodbc.connect(connection_string, autocommit=True)

query = 'DECLARE @IDs [dbo].IDTableType; ' \
          'INSERT INTO @IDs (ID) VALUES (1) ' \
          '{call [mydb].[dbo].deleteData(@IDs)};'
print('SQL DELETE QUERY:\n%s' % (query))
db_handle.execute(query)
```

When I run the following statement through pyodbc, the stored procedure winds up getting terminated partway through processing. So, I'll wind up with a number of the tables having their data cleared out and a number not. The number not cleared out isn't always the same, but it's never zero, so re-running the query numerous times never achieves the intended result. Perhaps worth noting, is that pyodbc doesn't fail or anything; the SQL statement's execution is aborted, but my python program continues to run fine (perhaps because I'm not capturing and examining some output...).

Intriguingly, if I remove the application resource lock blocks from around the delete statements in the stored procedure, pyodbc runs it to completion, as I expect. Also, just running the procedure (with the applock blocks) directly in SQL Server Management Studio works as I'd expect, as does running the stored procedure through other connections, like Perl's DBI module. I'm mostly curious as to whether I'm doing something wrong in my use of pyodbc, or whether this might actually be some bug. I've had a hard time finding relevant-looking problems and documentation in searches over the past couple days.

issue_title: Expose constants required to handle connection.getinfo(SQL_OJ_CAPABILITIES) result
issue_body:
From https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/sql/odbc/reference/syntax/sqlgetinfo-function `SQLGetInfo(SQL_OJ_CAPABILITIES)` returns bitmask of folllowing constants

```
SQL_OJ_LEFT = Left outer joins are supported.

SQL_OJ_RIGHT = Right outer joins are supported.

SQL_OJ_FULL = Full outer joins are supported.

SQL_OJ_NESTED = Nested outer joins are supported.

SQL_OJ_NOT_ORDERED = The column names in the ON clause of the outer join do not have to be in the same order as their respective table names in the OUTER JOIN clause.

SQL_OJ_INNER = The inner table (the right table in a left outer join or the left table in a right outer join) can also be used in an inner join. This does not apply to full outer joins, which do not have an inner table.

SQL_OJ_ALL_COMPARISON_OPS = The comparison operator in the ON clause can be any of the ODBC comparison operators. If this bit is not set, only the equals (=) comparison operator can be used in outer joins.
```

please expose the constants in `pyodbc` module.

issue_title: PostUpdate broken on python 2.7 when using str (byte) message
issue_body:
Hi, I've just upgraded to the last version 3.4 but I'm encountering the following issue:
I cannot longer do a PostUpdate in Python 2.7 when the message is a str(byte) string.
When using a unicode string, it works.

```
api = Api(consumer_key=CONSUMER_KEY,
          consumer_secret=CONSUMER_SECRET,
          access_token_key=TWITTERKEY,
          access_token_secret=TWITTERSECRET)

api.PostUpdate(message[:280])
```

I assume that you want to work with unicode string internally, but the code below does not seem to convert a byte string to a unicode string in all cases.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1945295/36450089-24aaf56c-168d-11e8-8470-3aeabdee6e74.png)

It seems that https://github.com/bear/python-twitter/blob/master/twitter/api.py#L1106-L1109 is only for Python 3 (where `str` is unicode). In Python 2 this is a byte string.
You may want to use the `six` library here to support both Python 2 and 3.

Since there is no conversion to unicode, it results in the following error:
````
File "C:\Tools\GitHub\Auto-Subliminal\lib\twitter\api.py", line 1111, in PostUpdate
    if verify_status_length and calc_expected_status_length(u_status) > CHARACTER_LIMIT:
  File "C:\Tools\GitHub\Auto-Subliminal\lib\twitter\twitter_utils.py", line 185, in calc_expected_status_length
    if any([ord(normalize("NFC", character)) in char_range for char_range in CHAR_RANGES]):
TypeError: must be unicode, not str
```

The transformation of a Python 3 string (Unicode) to bytes (encoded in what mecab is expecting) for input, and vice versa for output, is sort of a hack.

How about using a decorator instead?

issue_title: Issue with Repeat Rows when using pywin32
issue_body:
Hi, I'm using xlsxwriter to generate some workbooks.
When these workbooks are saved, I instantly reopened them with pywin32 and just save them.
I do this because xlsxwriter does not save the values of the formulas and I need these workbooks to be seen correctly from the mail app in iphone.
Opening them with the Excel runtime and immediately saving them stores the values of each formulas.

The problem here is that when I do this, the repeated rows set in xlsxwriter disappears.
I notice that the repeated rows disappear just before the open method of the pywin32 and not after the save.
```
excel_app = win32.gencache.EnsureDispatch("Excel.Application")
wb = excel_app.Workbooks.Open(file_path)
wb.Close(True)
```

I'm using windows and my system language is set to spanish.
The problem is:

In the workbook class in the method `_prepare_defined_names` the repeated rows are set as follows:
`defined_names.append(['_xlnm.Print_Titles',
                                      sheet.index, sheet_range, hidden])`

This "Print_Titles" are not recognised by my excel when used with pywin32 (however it is recognised when open manually with the excel app).

If I change this code in the workbook class to the spanish version (T├Łtulos_a_imprimir) it works perfectly (manually and with pywin32):

`defined_names.append(['_xlnm.T├Łtulos_a_imprimir',
                                      sheet.index, sheet_range, hidden])`

I don't know if this can be addressed in xlsxwriter as it seems to be some kind of problem with pywin32 but, I just want to notice it here and maybe found some workarounds.

Thanks in advance

issue_title: Consider using `placebo` for mocking AWS calls
issue_body:
There is a package (by Mitch Garnaat - initial author of `boto`) named [`placebo`](https://github.com/garnaat/placebo). It claims:

> Placebo allows you to mock boto3 calls that look just like normal calls but actually have no effect at all. It does this by allowing you to record a set of calls and save them to a data file and then replay those calls later (e.g. in a unit test) without ever hitting the AWS endpoints.

To me it looks like good candidate for replacing our current manually made AWS responses which are not complete in the content.

## Thinking of possible results

- using `placebo`, we once record calls to `awslogs` AWS service, save it and use later on in unit tests.
- our unittest would use `placebo` to create recorded AWS responses directly by "sending log records" to AWS. Recorded calls (and responses) would be used in unittest. Having this done with each test run would make much easier to modify log records and see they are really present.
- using `placebo` recorded calls we would create a fixture, which would build "recorded" responses from log records. This is somewhere in the half way between previous two options.

In all cases, we would be using some (really recorded or manually shaped) recorded calls/responses in our tests. The replay process would probably use `placebo`.

## Proposed steps

- initial exploration of `placebo` - does it work at all? How it records and how it replays calls.
- explore `placebo` for `awslogs` related calls. Does it meet our expectations? Does it work in this case at all?
- decide how to use `placebo`.

If all goes well, we could continue:

- replace existing in-line generated AWS responses by `placebo.pill`
- consider generating "recorded calls" at setup/fixture creation time to allow testing input log records to output log records.

issue_title: Electrum 3.1 unable to connect to electrumx
issue_body:
When I try to connect Electrum 3.1 on Mac or Android to my local electrumx, the Electrum client does not connect. Note that the earlier version 3.0.6 of Electrum client was working fine. In the electrumx log file, I see the following after an attempt to connect the Electrum client to electrumx -

INFO:BlockProcessor:flush #18,916 took 1.3s.  Height 512,490 txs: 303,451,008
INFO:BlockProcessor:processed 1 block in 47.2s
INFO:ElectrumX:[84] SSL 192.168.0.241:38222, 1 total
INFO:ElectrumX:[84] unsupported protocol version request 1.2
INFO:ElectrumX:[84] unsupported protocol version: 1.2
INFO:ElectrumX:[85] SSL 192.168.0.241:38241, 1 total
INFO:ElectrumX:[85] unsupported protocol version request 1.2
INFO:ElectrumX:[85] unsupported protocol version: 1.2

Any idea what has changed that is causing this?

The main reason for a new peer discovery mechanism is that Freenode doesn't want Electrum servers to be on their network.

A simple protocol could look like this:

1. Server `A` wants to join the network and connects to a list of hard-coded servers, including `B`. The JSONRPC interface  could be used for this.
2. Server `A` sends a command like `peer.add` containing information about `A`'s hostname, ports, pruning limit and optionally onion-address to `B` (and all the other servers).
2. Server `B` checks that the hostname it got from `A` resolves to the IP address `A` is connecting from. If it doesn't, it sends an error message. If it does, it responds with a list of all connected peers.
2. `A` continues to connect to the other known peers until it is connected to the whole network.

What do you think?

WeakFunctionProxy only proxies the original function, and ignores any patching done via wrapt.decorator.  Ex (tested in python 2.7):

```
import wrapt

@wrapt.decorator
def bark(wrapped, instance, args, kwargs):
    print 'The %s barks' % instance.__class__.__name__

class Animal(object):
    @bark
    def squeal(self):
        print 'The %s squeals' % self.__class__.__name__

animal = Animal()
animal.squeal()
```
Output: `The Animal barks`
```
weak_squeal = wrapt.WeakFunctionProxy(animal.squeal)
weak_squeal()
```
Output: `The Animal squeals`

Also, thank you for putting in the effort to write this package and explaining all the issues it addresses.  My decorators would be much more fragile without your help!

issue_title: apschedular not running for long interval
issue_body:
I'm using `apscheduler` to schedule cron task to run every hour. The script is hosted on `Heroku`
```
    from apscheduler.schedulers.blocking import BlockingScheduler
    sched = BlockingScheduler()
    
    @sched.scheduled_job('interval', minutes=60)
    def send_alert():
        # task logic
    
    sched.start()
```
**Procfile**
```
    clock: python telegram_alert.py
```
**But, it doesn't run. When I set `interval minutes` to `2` or `10`, it works fine. But setting it to a large value is not working.**

> Edit 2

I think cron is not running after 30 minutes. I changed duration to `minutes=10` to check how long does it run. It runs for 3 times only, means it run the task three times each on an interval of 10 minutes, after that It stopped running.

Is there some limitation with `scheduled_job`?

Following up on issue #146: Could it be that the misfire_grace_time is not saved and restored when using a sqlite jobstore?

On the next program start the misfire grace time is `1` for most jobs.

I actually found we had a bug in my code with a Date validator, which can be summarised as this:

```
In [63]: import voluptuous as V

In [64]: def Date(fmt='%Y-%m-%d'):
    ...:    return lambda v: datetime.strptime(v, fmt)
    ...: 

In [65]: V.Schema(Date)(11)
Out[65]: <function __main__.<lambda>>
```

So instead of calling Date I was just passing it.
Stupid mistake surely, but I wonder if voluptuos could at least warn about this.
Returning a function from the schema validation is normally not something that makes sense right?
When using the fluidsynth methods to play different containers, what does changing the channel do?

If you try to set a SQLAlchemy `DateTime` value using SQLite as the backing database, you will get
````
sqlalchemy.exc.StatementError
StatementError: SQLite Date type only accepts Python date objects as input.
````

Since you cannot really import `datetime` and set this in the json directly it be handled as the object is created

`````
import datetime
created = datetime.utcnow()
cod = Code(created=created)
db.session.add(cod)
db.session.commit()
``````

Or using a separate setter function.

Again this is a gotcha with SQLite that is up to you to either warn people about or provide a way for them to be set.

Each statement object that ChatterBot stores contains an attribute for referencing each statement that it has been used to respond to.

issue_title: UbuntuCorpusTrainer fails on non-existent "data/ubuntu_dialogs" directory after extraction.
issue_body:
So, using the example from `ChatterBot/examples/ubuntu_corpus_training_example.py`, it seems that the `ubuntu_dialogs.tgz` archive is downloaded and extracted successfully, however, the bot doesn't seem to be picking responses from it after `.train()` and also seems to want to extract the archive on every run. **ubuntu_corpus_training_example.py with comments removed:**
```python
from chatterbot import ChatBot
import logging

logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)

chatbot = ChatBot(
    'Example Bot',
    trainer='chatterbot.trainers.UbuntuCorpusTrainer'
)

chatbot.train()

response = chatbot.get_response('How are you doing today?')
print(response)

issue_title: No valid parse on lexical ambiguity
issue_body:
* parglare version: 0.5
* Python version: 2.7.13
* Operating System: macOS 10.13.4

### Description

I'm migrating some basic NLP to parglare to test it, and bumped into this with some obscure regexes. It's probably not a happy case to have a space there, but I wanted to report it anyway. Here's a minimal version:

    grammar = r"""
    S: FOO | BAR;
    
    FOO: /a/ "foo";
    
    BAR: /a / "bar";
    """

    g = Grammar.from_string(grammar)
    GLRParser(g).parse('a foo')

This raises a ParseError: Error at position 1,2 => "a *foo". Expected: bar

I'm using the GLR parser since I need all possible parses (not that it would be useful with that example). Is there any way to get it to parse, without registering custom_lexical_disambiguation? (in case those regex recognizers are converted to literal strings, I don't even get the chance to fix it in the registered disambiguator)

Currently, static disambiguation filters/rules are defined per production using `{}` syntax.

This issue will deal with defining the same at the grammar rule level. Production level filters should be "stronger" and override those defined at rule level.

issue_title: `ValueError` when running the GeneralOptimizer with a `Pyramid` topology
issue_body:
**Describe the bug**
The `Pyramid` topology throws the following error when ran in the `GeneralOptimizerPSO` (only in some cases):
```python
\AppData\Local\Programs\Python\Python37-32\lib\site-packages\src\pyswarms\pyswarms\backend\topology\pyramid.py", line 75, in <listcomp>
    [swarm.pbest_cost[self.neighbor_idx[i]].argmin() for i in range(len(self.neighbor_idx))]
ValueError: attempt to get argmin of an empty sequence
```

**To Reproduce**
Steps to reproduce the behaviour:

- Run this script in the current `development` version:
```python
import pyswarms as ps
import numpy as np
from pyswarms.utils.functions import single_obj as fx
from pyswarms.backend.topology import Random, Pyramid, Ring, Star, VonNeumann

# Set-up hyperparameters
options = {"c1": 2, "c2": 2, "w": 0.5, "k": 4, "p": 2, "r":1}
iters = 1000
dim = 5
bounds = (np.repeat(-5.12, dim), np.repeat(5.12, dim))
topology = Pyramid(static=False)
optimizer = ps.single.GeneralOptimizerPSO(n_particles=10, dimensions=dim, options=options, bounds=bounds, topology=topology)
best_cost, best_pos = optimizer.optimize(fx.sphere_func, iters=iters, verbose=0, print_step=1)
```

**Environment:**
 - OS: Windows
 - PySwarms Version development
 - Python Version 3.7.0

**Additional context and bug fix**
I investigated this bug and it turns out this might be a precision error in the `Delaunay` class which is used in the `Pyramid` topology to compute the neighbours. It seems that if two particles are too close to each other the triangulation isn't computed correctly. This leads to some particles not having any neighbours, that is, their array in the `neighbour_idx` attribute being empty. I looked up the [scipy documentation](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy-0.15.1/reference/generated/scipy.spatial.Delaunay.html) which led me to the [qhull documentation](http://www.qhull.org/html/qdelaun.htm). I suggest that we change this line:

```python
pyramid = Delaunay(swarm.position)
```

to the following:

```python
pyramid = Delaunay(swarm.position, qhull_options="QJ0.001 Qbb Qc Qx")
```
What the options do:

- `QJ0.001`: randomly joggle particles in the range [-0.001,0.001], that is, the particles are randomly pushed apart if they are in too narrow of a space. This makes sure that the particles don't get too close together.
- `Qbb`: improves numeric precision. I'm not sure if this is necessary but I included it because it is labelled as default in the scipy doc.
- `Qc`: keep coplanar points with the nearest facet. I'm not exactly sure what this means, but it's in the defaults so I included it.
- `Qx`: exact pre-merges (allows coplanar facets). Again not sure what it does but it's in the defaults.

The `Qz` option (also in the defaults), together with the 4 above, leads to a `MemoryError`.
For more precise information visit [this page](http://www.qhull.org/html/qh-optq.htm#Qc).

I've run the `optimize` method about 300 times to make sure this fixes the error. And it apparently does! ­¤śä 

I can do a separate PR for this or I can include it in the cleanup PR I've already made. What do you think, @ljvmiranda921?

issue_title: 0.26 is broken on Windows (fails to initialize the random number generator)
issue_body:
When check-manifest 0.26 tries to run `python setup.py sdist` in a subprocess, it gets this error on Python 2:
```
WindowsError: [Error -2146893795] Provider DLL failed to initialize correctly
```
and the equivalent error on Python 3:
```
Fatal Python error: Failed to initialize Windows random API (CryptoGen)

This is caused by executing the Python subprocess with an empty(ish) environment (commit 396fda84345942526e7cf518aed3d87192c23d56).  See http://bugs.python.org/issue20614, https://code.djangoproject.com/ticket/24160 for details.

My Jenkins [caught this](https://jenkins.gedmin.as/job/check-manifest-on-windows/355/console).

issue_title: diff-quality checks only the first modified file
issue_body:
Hi,

When running `diff-quality --violations pyflakes --compare-branch master` (i.e. letting diff-quality run pyflakes itself), only the first modified file is checked.

To reproduce:
```
git clone git@github.com:fperrin/diff-cover-issue-demo.git
cd diff-cover-issue-demo/
git checkout check_only_first 
diff-quality --violations pyflakes 
```
This reports only the issue in hello.py, but if you `git diff master..` it's obvious you should have a warning in hi.py too.

I think the problem is in violationsreporter.base.QualityReporter.violations; after checking the first file, self.reports is set, so when violations() is called on the second file, it does nothing.

```python
@contextlib.contextmanager
def set_default_encoding(encoding='ascii'):
    """\
    Temporarily set the default encoding within the span of the context.

    NOTE: Using `sys.setdefaultencoding()` is HIGHLY DISCOURAGED and this context
    manager should only be used when working with third party libraries that incorrectly
    handle unicode/strings/bytestrings and cannot easily be patched, updated, or worked around.
    """
    import sys
    reload(sys)
    original_encoding = sys.getdefaultencoding()
    sys.setdefaultencoding(encoding)
    yield
    sys.setdefaultencoding(original_encoding)

issue_title: IndexError when you get an empty buffer
issue_body:
With an SSH session active, reboot or disable networking on the target machine (to close the remote end of the SSH session). Then, try to use paramiko-expect to scan through the shell. I would expect a socket.timeout, but instead you get an IndexError.

This is because the buffer is empty (on retrieval from remote connection), then you try to scan through the current output for the given regex strings, then try to directly access a 'found_pattern' that isn't actually found. 

This could by solved by either converting the While loop to a While: Else (for when the buffer breaks out of the loop), or checking that there is actually a found pattern.

issue_title: filter_complex should not be used for basic pipelines
issue_body:
I'm trying to use ffmpeg-python for a very simple task, concatenating many mp3s together.
I set up my pipeline like so:

```
import ffmpeg
import os
import os.path

audio_files = sorted(os.listdir("."))[:5]
audio_inputs = list(map(lambda audio_file: ffmpeg.input(audio_file), audio_files))
ffmpeg.concat(*audio_inputs).output("concat_test.mp3").run()
```

Which produces the command:

```
ffmpeg -i "file-0" -i "file-1" -i "file2" -i "file3" -i "file4" -filter_complex [0][1][2][3][4]concat=n=5[s0] -map [s0] concat_test.mp3
```

This fails with the error message: 
Stream specifier '' in filtergraph description [0][1][2][3][4]concat=n=5[s0] matches no streams.

However if I remove the -filter_complex and -map flags then the command runs as expected.

Hello, 

First I would like to appreciate your work on creating logger plugin that simplifies the logging job when running in a large test environment.
 
I'm trying to use your plugin for my test cases logging. As per its design, it works perfectly so this is not a bug report. However, I have a use case that I would like to see if this plugin can support as a new feature. 

My use case is for some of the testcase I want to redirect logs to the file other than what default, that is being created by this plugin. Let's take an example.

I'm adding the file logging for "app/testcases/test_app.py", which has two test cases under a class MYTESTS,  test_foo and test_bar. Now as per existing plugin features the log files will get created like this.

/tmp/pytest-of-rajiv/logs/app/testcases/test_app/MYTEST.test_foo/pytest_logger
and
/tmp/pytest-of-rajiv/logs/app/testcases/test_app/MYTEST.test_bar/pytest_logger

However, for MYTEST.test_bar I want to redirect the log file to some other location with the different name like this.

/tharnlog/custer1/20180106/test_app/MYTEST.test_bar.log

This something I should be able to control using pytest_test_setup hook based on certain markers.

Currently, the plugin uses nodeid to create the filehandler which will be the by default getting created using full file path from the root directory. There is no option to modify the file path except modifying the --logger-logsdir. Basically, redirect the log file to some other location other than default filehandler. 

After going through your code I feel that it is possible to do it by modifying the LoggerState object and pass it to make handler. 
```

class LoggerPlugin(object):
    def __init__(self, config, logcfg):
        self._config = config
        self._logdirlinks = config.hook.pytest_logger_logdirlink(config=config)
        self._loggers = _loggers_from_logcfg(logcfg, config.getoption('log')) if logcfg._enabled else None
        self._formatter_class = logcfg._formatter_class or DefaultFormatter
        self._logsdir = None
        self._redirectpath = None

```
Modify the state object by influencing this hook. I did the hookwrapper in my conftest to make sure my hook gets called first and I can influence the loggerstate. 

```
    def pytest_runtest_setup(self, item):
        loggers = _choose_loggers(self._loggers, _loggers_from_hooks(item))
        formatter = self._formatter_class()
        item._logger = state = LoggerState(item=item,
                                           stdoutloggers=loggers.stdout,
                                           fileloggers=loggers.file,
                                           formatter=formatter,
                                           redirectpath=self._redirectpath)
        state.on_setup()
```
```
class LoggerState(object):
    def __init__(self, item, stdoutloggers, fileloggers, formatter, redirectpath=None):
        self._put_newlines = bool(item.config.option.capture == 'no' and stdoutloggers)
        self.handlers = _make_handlers(stdoutloggers, fileloggers, item, formatter, redirectpath)
        self.root_enabler = RootEnabler(bool(stdoutloggers and fileloggers))
```
```
def _make_logdir(item, redirectpath=None):
    plugin = item.config.pluginmanager.getplugin('_logger')
    if (redirectpath):
        return plugin.logsdir().join(redirectpath).ensure(dir=1)
    else:
        return plugin.logsdir().join(_sanitize_nodeid(item.nodeid)).ensure(dir=1)

```
```
def _make_handlers(stdoutloggers, fileloggers, item, formatter, redirectpath=None):
    handlers = []
    if stdoutloggers:
        handlers += _make_stdout_handlers(stdoutloggers, formatter)
    if fileloggers:
        logdir = _make_logdir(item, redirectpath)
        handlers += _make_file_handlers(fileloggers, formatter, logdir)
    return handlers

```

And this is how we can influence this hook from conftest 

```
@pytest.hookimpl(hookwrapper=True)
def pytest_runtest_setup(item):

    # Let's get the logger plugin
    logger = item.config.pluginmanager.getplugin('_logger')

    #add a File logger
    testname, filename = get_test_file_name(item.nodeid)

    filename = os.path.splitext(filename)[0]
    testnames = (testname, logging.DEBUG)
    logger._loggers.file = [(testnames)]
    newpath = filename
    logger._redirectpath = newpath

    outcome = yield

```
I find this use case is very useful and generic because many of the times we want to record the logging to commonplace for specific reasons. For me, the log files are generated from various test systems in a huge test infrastructure setup. That setup is already generating log files with that naming convention. I can write something as post-test teardown that will copy the log file into that location but that will not be real time and slow down my testing.

I would greatly appreciate your input. I'm also ready to contribute under your guidance.

Thanks,
Rajiv Gupta

issue_title: A failing autodiscover request should invalidate the domain name cache
issue_body:
Repeatedly getting this traceback, but we should invalidate the cache and retry if get get AutoDiscoverFailed in this code path.

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "test.py", line 24, in <module>
    a = Account(primary_smtp_address=email, credentials=c, access_type=DELEGATE, autodiscover=True, verify_ssl=False)
  File "/lib/python3.4/site-packages/exchangelib/account.py", line 43, in __init__
    credentials=credentials, verify_ssl=verify_ssl)
  File "/lib/python3.4/site-packages/exchangelib/autodiscover.py", line 155, in discover
    protocol=protocol)
  File "/lib/python3.4/site-packages/exchangelib/autodiscover.py", line 296, in _autodiscover_quick
    r = _get_autodiscover_response(protocol=protocol, email=email)
  File "/lib/python3.4/site-packages/exchangelib/autodiscover.py", line 354, in _get_autodiscover_response
    raise AutoDiscoverFailed('No access to %s using %s' % (protocol.service_endpoint, protocol.auth_type))
exchangelib.errors.AutoDiscoverFailed: No access to https://autodiscover.example.com/Autodiscover/Autodiscover.xml using NTLM
```

Disabling SSL verification is strongly discouraged and should not be this easy :-) Also, there are a huge number of connection customizations the user may want to do. `requests` suggests custom `HTTPAdapter` classes to handle this customization, and we support registering custom classes.

Let's remove the `verify_ssl` options in favour of:

```python
from requests.adapters import HTTPAdapter
from exchangelib.protocol import BaseProtocol

class NoVerifyHTTPAdapter(HTTPAdapter):
    def init_poolmanager(self, *args, **kwargs):
        kwargs['verify'] = False
        super(NoVerifyHTTPAdapter, self).init_poolmanager(*args, **kwargs)

BaseProtocol.HTTP_ADAPTER_CLS = NoVerifyHTTPAdapter
```

We can even provide `NoVerifyHTTPAdapter` for convenience.

Remember to keep `verify_ssl: False` support in the test suite.

issue_title: [bug] load_image_file can't load some bytes broken image
issue_body:
I just want to know whether the image has faces. So, I use the code:
```
import face_recognition
img = face_recognition.load_image_file(imgname, mode='RGB')
faces = face_recognition.face_locations(img)
```
Suddenly, it raise error, the img must be uint8 or a PIL image.

I saw you use the function ```scipy.misc.imread()``` function to read image.
It's a combination of ```scipy.misc.fromimage(), PIL.Image.open()```.
If an image has some bytes broken, the ```scipy.misc.fromimage()```gets unexpected result.

After my endeaver, you just to add 
```
from PIL import ImageFile
ImageFile.LOAD_TRUNCATED_IMAGES = True
```
And everything goes perfect.
more details tomorrow.

* face_recognition version: the lastest version
* Python version: python 2.7
* Operating System: MacOS

### Description

As the example script of facerec_from_webcam_faster is for one person, I wonder how to do face recognition for multi-people like the README.md showed.

issue_title: Uploading .gz file to S3 results in file being stored uncompressed
issue_body:
I have compressed log files I'm trying to store in S3 using django-storages s3 backend, but when I inspect the files I discover that S3 stored them in their uncompressed form.  I have done some digging and discovered that django-storages is properly identifying my files as gzipped, but setting that as a ContentEncoding argument so that S3 interprets the data as gzip for HTTP transfer encoding and uncompresses it at the HTTP layer at put time.

Content encoding detection:
https://github.com/jschneier/django-storages/blob/master/storages/backends/s3boto3.py#L421-L433
In the _save() method encoding is detected with mimetypes.guess_type() which in my case results in 'gzip'.

If I were to set AWS_IS_GZIPPED and add my gzip file type to GZIP_CONTENT_TYPES then it is going to attempt to gzip my data a second time for transfer when self._compress_content() is called.  This feels undesirable with 100MB compressed log files

The bucket object `obj` that is created is used to call upload_fileobj with the ContentEncoding parameter which eventually calls Client.put_object with ContentEncoding set to gzip
http://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/services/s3.html#S3.Client.put_object

I can create a pull request, that would fix this for my use case, but I would like to understand what is expected and what option is least likely to break other people's use cases.
Options for fixing this:

1. Detect the .gz file extension and do not set the ContentEncoding value
2. Disable ContentEncoding detection entirely if AWS_IS_GZIPPED == False - Is there a use case for locally compressed assets like stylesheets that need to be decompressed on S3?
3. Other options I haven't thought of?

If you have any thoughts on what kind of approach you would like to see taken I can take them and get a pull request submitted for review.

issue_title: Cleanse references to this being a fork
issue_body:
I think now that this repository is being published via django-storages, its time to start cleaning up the documentation that references this as a fork.  It was very confusing to read that this is (was) a fork, and have the install instructions pointing to django-storages.

To avoid confusing new / returning users to this project I think a small paragraph explaining what happened and that we now have control over the namespace again would be a major improvement.  Preferably in really big bold letters.

issue_title: apache_libcloud.py url() function is not idempotent
issue_body:
In apache_libcloud.py, the url() method is not idempotent, as it is for the s3boto driver.

This is problematic because it results in the remote API being hit for URLs that are not files, for example, this usage is valid: 

The code in question is at line 127:

```
   def url(self, name):
        provider_type = self.provider['type'].lower()
        obj = self._get_object(name)
        if not obj:
            return None
        try:
            url = self.driver.get_object_cdn_url(obj)
        except NotImplementedError as e:
            object_path = '%s/%s' % (self.bucket, obj.name)
            if 's3' in provider_type:
                base_url = 'https://%s' % self.driver.connection.host
                url = urljoin(base_url, object_path)
            elif 'google' in provider_type:
                url = urljoin('https://storage.googleapis.com', object_path)
            elif 'azure' in provider_type:
                base_url = ('https://%s.blob.core.windows.net' %
                            self.provider['user'])
                url = urljoin(base_url, object_path)
            else:
                raise e
        return url
```

If the driver implements get_object_cdn_url, then the API object _may_ be required. (Though the atmos driver defaults to not using the object, for example). But if the driver does not implement that method, then the call to self._get_object(name), and the corresponding API round-trip,  were unnecessary. This is true in the case of the GoogleStorageDriver, for example, which does not implement get_object_cdn_url.

In addition to a performance overhead, this actually breaks some code. For example, see https://github.com/marcgibbons/django-rest-swagger/blob/master/rest_framework_swagger/templates/rest_framework_swagger/base.html#L50.

It's valid to call url() through template code like `{% static '' %}`, which would be expected to get the base URL of the storage, and would not generally be expected to result in a storage API call. However, under the current code path, (and after working around https://github.com/jschneier/django-storages/issues/132 to generate a valid URL), the library attempts to retrieve `https://storage.googleapis.com/bucket-name/` as an object, which fails because Google Storage doesn't set the ETag header on the response for the root directory.

This is going to be somewhat disruptive to fix, I suspect -- perhaps get_object_cdn_url could be extracted into a mixin, so you can do

```
if isinstance(self, CDNStorageMixin):
    obj = self._get_object(name)
    self.get_object_cdn_url(obj)
else:
    #existing logic
```

Got this error:-

```
ImportError: No module named 'storages.backends.s3boto3'
```
Checking the tarball, it indeed missing - https://pypi.python.org/packages/f4/f3/d6c91114441eaf3a0d00029ff214e2ab7fff4a4e7a1cfa57fe12f6eed992/django-storages-redux-1.3.2.tar.gz

issue_title: errors in CTV when start = stop = 0

issue_body:
I ran into this accidentally while trying to recreate the stepped-style plot elsewhere and found that the value passed for `flat` seemed to be ignored, and the resulting plot is always stepped/flat.

In case it was related to the data I was using, I recreated it with the political regimes dataset from `lifelines.datasets.load_dd()`. Below are snippets of the plot command used for each resulting graph in the image.

``` python
data = lifelines.datasets.load_dd()
kmf = lifelines.KaplanMeierFitter()
kmf.fit(data['duration'], data['observed'])
kmf.plot(ci_show=False, show_censors=True, flat=False, title='Flat False')  # title lengthened later
kmf.plot(ci_show=False, show_censors=True, flat=True, title='Flat True')  # title lengthened later
```

I dug into it a bit myself, but don't see that `plt.step()` is even ever called; in fact `matplotlib.pyplot.step()` defaults its `where` parameter to `'pre'` and I had to explicitly call `step(..., where='post')` to get it to match the default results from `kmf.plot()`. My only guess is whether it's related to calling `fill_between()` like an unintended side effect or something. 

![screen shot 2016-09-23 at 6 09 37 pm](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1156198/18802923/eb88cc6e-81b9-11e6-8357-c9e647935b00.png)

issue_title: Predictions using the Cox time varying fitter.
issue_body:
Hello @CamDavidsonPilon,

First of all, thanks a lot for providing us with the library!

My question is regarding making predictions using the Cox Time varying fitter. There is very little documentation about the issue. 

My problem is that in CoxPH, we do not need to specify the time at which the covariates were measured as they are assumed to be constant over the whole observation period, for each individual. However, for the time varying method, it does make a difference to know at which point in time our covariates were measured.

Do we thus have to provide the start and stop time in our dataset in the .predict() method?

Thanks a lot,
Tom

issue_title: Error when lint outfile path not exists
issue_body:
When specifying outfile with intermediate dirs like
```sh
python setup.py lint --lint-packages my.package --lint-output reports/lint.out
```
the command aborts with an error
```sh
error: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: 'reports/lint.out'
```
As a workaround, in each `setup.py` using linting I override the `PylintCommand`:

```python
import os
...
from setuptools_lint.setuptools_command import PylintCommand

class PylintCommandPatched(PylintCommand):
    
    def finalize_options(self):
        if self.lint_output:
            out_dir = os.path.dirname(self.lint_output)
            os.makedirs(out_dir, exist_ok=True)
        PylintCommand.finalize_options(self)

setup(
      ...
      cmdclass={
          'lint': PylintCommandPatched,
      },
      ...
)
```

Maybe it's possible to create missing dirs on outfile path in the `PylintCommand`?

issue_title: Error if the path to the socket is greater than 70ish characters
issue_body:
I ran into an issue where I had a path to a socket that looked like this:

```
 %2Fstorage%2Ftestroot%2F123456789%2F123456789111%2Fdata%2Flocal_auth.sock
```

Which is 73 characters long. When prefixed with `http+unix`, the full path looks like:

```
 http+unix://%2Fstorage%2Ftestroot%2F123456789%2F123456789111%2Fdata%2Flocal_auth.sock
```

When passed to requests, the following error occurs:

> File "/usr/local/updated-openssl/lib/python3.4/site-packages/requests_unixsocket/**init**.py", line 60, in post
> File "/usr/local/updated-openssl/lib/python3.4/site-packages/requests_unixsocket/**init**.py", line 46, in request
> File "/usr/local/updated-openssl/lib/python3.4/site-packages/requests/sessions.py", line 461, in request
> File "/usr/local/updated-openssl/lib/python3.4/site-packages/requests/sessions.py", line 394, in prepare_request
> File "/usr/local/updated-openssl/lib/python3.4/site-packages/requests/models.py", line 295, in prepare
> File "/usr/local/updated-openssl/lib/python3.4/site-packages/requests/models.py", line 364, in prepare_url
> requests.exceptions.InvalidURL: URL has an invalid label.

Which is in turn triggered by this:

> Traceback (most recent call last):
> File "/usr/local/updated-openssl/lib/python3.4/site-packages/requests/models.py", line 362, in prepare_url
> UnicodeError: encoding with 'idna' codec failed (UnicodeError: label empty or too long)

Digging around in the source for `models.py`, it looks like it extracts the host (which is the path to the socket above), and then performs:

`host = host.encode('idna').decode('utf-8')`

This will fail in accordance with the IDNA RFC, which states:

```
The conversions between ASCII and non-ASCII forms of a domain name are accomplished by algorithms called ToASCII and ToUnicode. These algorithms are not applied to the domain name as a whole, but rather to individual labels. For example, if the domain name is www.example.com, then the labels are www, example, and com. ToASCII or ToUnicode are applied to each of these three separately.

The details of these two algorithms are complex, and are specified in RFC 3490. The following gives an overview of their function.

ToASCII leaves unchanged any ASCII label, but will fail if the label is unsuitable for the Domain Name System. If given a label containing at least one non-ASCII character, ToASCII will apply the Nameprep algorithm, which converts the label to lowercase and performs other normalization, and will then translate the result to ASCII using Punycode[16] before prepending the four-character string "xn--".[17] This four-character string is called the ASCII Compatible Encoding (ACE) prefix, and is used to distinguish Punycode encoded labels from ordinary ASCII labels. The ToASCII algorithm can fail in several ways; for example, the final string could exceed the 63-character limit of a DNS name. A label for which ToASCII fails cannot be used in an internationalized domain name.
```

My interpretation of the error is that if the path to the socket is rather long, this fails. Is there a suggested workaround other than symlinking the socket file to something short?

issue_title: In Python 3 .iteritims in __init__.py break code; solution provided

Maarten Zaanen

I'm having some trouble trying to understand the idea with the ``determine_path`` method: https://github.com/reinout/z3c.dependencychecker/blob/master/z3c/dependencychecker/dependencychecker.py#L577

If I pass another folder, i.e.
```
git clone z3c.dependencychecker (virtualenv and what not)
git clone plone.scale

cd z3c.dependencychecker
./bin/dependencychecker -v ../plone.api
```

With that I get the following output:
```
DEBUG: Looking in directory ../plone.scale, passed on the command line
DEBUG: No imported modules found.
DEBUG: No imported modules found.
DEBUG: All found regular imported packages: []
DEBUG: All found regular imported test packages: []
DEBUG: Appended ourselves (z3c.dependencychecker) to the required packages.
DEBUG: All found zcml-related packages: []
DEBUG: All found zcml-related test packages: []
DEBUG: All found FTI-related packages: []
DEBUG: All found FTI-related test packages: []
DEBUG: All found django_settings-related packages: []
DEBUG: All found django_settings-related test packages: []
DEBUG: All found generic_setup-related packages: []
DEBUG: All found generic_setup-related test packages: []
Unneeded test requirements
==========================
     pytest

Note: requirements are taken from the egginfo dir, so you need
to re-run buildout (or setup.py or whatever) for changes in
setup.py to have effect.
```

As you can see on the output it complains about ``pytest`` (which is not in plone.scale but rather on z3c.dependencychecker) and it also adds ``z3c.depenencychecker`` rather than plone.scale as the own package:
```
DEBUG: Appended ourselves (z3c.dependencychecker) to the required packages.
```

This last error I think comes from ``name_from_setup`` as there we call ``python setup.py --name`` without taking into account the, possibly, given path as a command line argument.

The former error, pytest being reported, probably is somewhat related? We analyze ``requires.txt`` from where we call rather than from where the argument was given?

Bottom line is, what was the purpose here, to do:
-  ``./bin/dependencychecker ../plone.scale`` or
- ``cd ../plone.scale && ../z3c.dependencychecker/bin/dependencychecker``

The later works, the former does not.

IMHO I would favor the former approach (pass the root path of the source checkout where setup.py should be found) and if not given assume ``os.getcwd()`` and march forward with the analysis.

@reinout @mauritsvanrees ideas, comments? ­¤śā

z3c.dependencychecker is not slow per se,  but both implementations have a shared problem:  they keep scanning the filesystem for files with possible imports.

On the new implementation for example that happens for every single class in modules.py.

I see two different approaches on optimizing this:

- use VCS information (we should add another warning about it then)
- use MANIFEST.in information (again a new warning would be useful)

The big downside would be testing, either approach would make tests a bit more complex, though with a pytest fixture the solution can be elegant enough to not be too cumbersome.

Ideas? Should that be a real concern? ­¤żö

    class SwaggerSchemaView(APIView):
        permission_classes = [AllowAny,]

issue_title: Provide an API for detecting invalid JSON Pointers
issue_body:
Hi!

Would you consider an API to validate whether a particular input was valid under RFC 6901 (or does such a thing exist already and I've missed it)?

E.g., `/foo/bar~` is not a valid JSON Pointer seemingly (the ~ is not escaped), but I cannot see an API that complains about that -- jsonpointer.JsonPointer will happily accept it as another pointer part.

(Even having jsonpointer.JsonPointer do enough validation of its inputs would also work).

Full context: JSON Schema Draft 6 adds a [jsonpointer format](http://json-schema.org/latest/json-schema-validation.html#rfc.section.8.3.9). I'd love to use python-json-pointer to implement it in [jsonschema](https://github.com/Julian/jsonschema).

Is a REST API around apscheduler not coming with the risk of CSRF attacks, considering that someone could add a job by having you click on a link which sends a HTTP POST request to add a job? https://github.com/viniciuschiele/flask-apscheduler/blob/master/flask_apscheduler/views.py#L35-L46 ?
I'd allow access to flask-apscheduler only from localhost, but it might not be sufficient (see: https://lwn.net/Articles/703485/), if you just click a bad link, which knows about your flask-apscheduler service and adds a job to it. If you don't run a webbrowser on your production server you're fine, because then you don't click on any links, but maybe it should still be considered?

A workaround could be to add jobs via a configuration file (so you are secured by linux file permissions) and then send a REST API request to reload jobs from the configuration file?

Maybe also adding authentication to HTTP requests could improve security?

    def __str__(self):
        if self.parent is None:

issue_title: Two project in same instance EC2
issue_body:
I have an EC2 server with redis, when I run two django projects on the same server "environment prod and dev with its virtual independent enviroments" in the dev domain the websocket is not able to join the groups in this environment and in the configuration of channels I have the prefix and everything

You can run two different projects sharing the same daphne server and redis instance

Thank you so much

Hello,

I see on Pypi that the new release of django-contrib-comments (1.5.1) has only be done on the Wheel format. 

It's cool for a faster installation, but in my case, my package installer (zc.buildout) does not support Wheel packages.

For a better compatibility, could you make a standard source release ?

Regards

issue_title: MNIST LeNet forward fails on master branch
issue_body:
MNIST LeNet forward fails on master branch. @eric-haibin-lin mentioned that it may be related to this PR https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/pull/10911
```python
import mxnet as mx
from mxnet.gluon import nn, Block
net = nn.HybridSequential()
with net.name_scope():
        net.add(nn.Conv2D(in_channels=1, channels=20, kernel_size=5))
        net.add(nn.Activation('relu'))
        net.add(nn.MaxPool2D(pool_size=2, strides=2))
        net.add(nn.Conv2D(in_channels=20, channels=50, kernel_size=5))
        net.add(nn.Activation('relu'))
        net.add(nn.MaxPool2D(pool_size=2, strides=2))
        # The Flatten layer collapses all axis, except the first one, into one axis.
        net.add(nn.Flatten())
        net.add(nn.Dense(512,in_units=800))
        net.add(nn.Activation('relu'))
        net.add(nn.Dense(10, in_units=512))
net.collect_params().initialize()
x = mx.random.uniform(shape=(8, 1, 28, 28))
net(x)
```
Got this error
```bash
Segmentation fault (core dumped)
```

i am doing something about semantic segmentation and use network definition below:

    data = mx.sym.Variable('data')
    # VGG part
    # group 1
    conv1_1 = mx.sym.Convolution(data=data, kernel=(3, 3),num_filter=64, name='conv1_1', workspace=2048)
    relu1_1= mx.sym.Activation(data=conv1_1, act_type='relu', name='relu1_1')
    conv1_2 = mx.sym.Convolution(data=relu1_1, kernel=(3, 3), num_filter=64, name='conv1_2', workspace=2048)
    relu1_2= mx.sym.Activation(data=conv1_2, act_type='relu', name='relu1_2')
    pool1 = mx.sym.Pooling(data=relu1_2, pool_type='max', kernel=(2, 2),  stride=(2, 2), name='pool1')

    # group 2
    conv2_1 = mx.sym.Convolution(data=pool1, kernel=(3, 3),num_filter=128, name='conv2_1', workspace=2048)
    relu2_1= mx.sym.Activation(data=conv2_1, act_type='relu', name='relu2_1')
    conv2_2 = mx.sym.Convolution(data=relu2_1, kernel=(3, 3), num_filter=128, name='conv2_2', workspace=2048)
    relu2_2= mx.sym.Activation(data=conv2_2, act_type='relu', name='relu2_2')
    pool2 = mx.sym.Pooling(data=relu2_2, pool_type='max', kernel=(2, 2),  stride=(2, 2), name='pool2')

    # group 3
    conv3_1 = mx.sym.Convolution(data=pool2, kernel=(3, 3),num_filter=256, name='conv3_1', workspace=2048)
    relu3_1= mx.sym.Activation(data=conv3_1, act_type='relu', name='relu3_1')
    conv3_2 = mx.sym.Convolution(data=relu3_1, kernel=(3, 3), num_filter=256, name='conv3_2', workspace=2048)
    relu3_2= mx.sym.Activation(data=conv3_2, act_type='relu', name='relu3_2')
    conv3_3 = mx.sym.Convolution(data=relu3_2, kernel=(3, 3), num_filter=256, name='conv3_3', workspace=2048)
    relu3_3= mx.sym.Activation(data=conv3_3, act_type='relu', name='relu3_3')
    pool3 = mx.sym.Pooling(data=relu3_3, pool_type='max', kernel=(2, 2),  stride=(2, 2), name='pool3')

    # group 4 without pool4
    conv4_1 = mx.sym.Convolution(data=pool3, kernel=(3, 3),num_filter=512, name='conv4_1', workspace=2048)
    relu4_1= mx.sym.Activation(data=conv4_1, act_type='relu', name='relu4_1')
    conv4_2 = mx.sym.Convolution(data=relu4_1, kernel=(3, 3), num_filter=512, name='conv4_2', workspace=2048)
    relu4_2= mx.sym.Activation(data=conv4_2, act_type='relu', name='relu4_2')
    conv4_3 = mx.sym.Convolution(data=relu4_2, kernel=(3, 3), num_filter=512, name='conv4_3', workspace=2048)
    relu4_3= mx.sym.Activation(data=conv4_3, act_type='relu', name='relu4_3')

    # dilation part
    conv5_1 = mx.sym.Convolution(data=relu4_3, kernel=(3, 3), num_filter=512, dilate=(2, 2), name='conv5_1', workspace=2048)
    relu5_1 = mx.sym.Activation(data=conv5_1, act_type='relu', name='relu5_1')
    conv5_2 = mx.sym.Convolution(data=relu5_1, kernel=(3, 3), num_filter=512, dilate=(2, 2), name='conv5_2', workspace=2048)
    relu5_2 = mx.sym.Activation(data=conv5_2, act_type='relu', name='relu5_2')
    conv5_3 = mx.sym.Convolution(data=relu5_2, kernel=(3, 3), num_filter=512, dilate=(2, 2), name='conv5_3', workspace=2048)
    relu5_3 = mx.sym.Activation(data=conv5_3, act_type='relu', name='relu5_3')

    fc6 = mx.sym.Convolution(data=relu5_3, kernel=(7, 7), num_filter=4096, dilate=(4, 4), name='fc6', workspace=2048)
    relu6 = mx.sym.Activation(data=fc6, act_type='relu', name='relu6')
    drop6 = mx.sym.Dropout(data=relu6, p=0.5, name='drop6')
    fc7 = mx.sym.Convolution(data=drop6, kernel=(1, 1), num_filter=4096, name='fc7', workspace=2048)
    relu7 = mx.sym.Activation(data=fc7, act_type='relu', name='relu7')
    drop7 = mx.sym.Dropout(data=relu7, p=0.5, name='drop7')

    final = mx.sym.Convolution(data=drop7, kernel=(1, 1), num_filter=num_class, name='final', workspace=2048)
    ctx_conv1_1 = mx.sym.Convolution(data=final, kernel=(3, 3), pad=(1, 1), num_filter=num_class, name='ctx_conv1_1', workspace=2048)
    ctx_relu1_1 = mx.sym.Activation(data=ctx_conv1_1, act_type='relu', name='ctx_relu1_1')
    ctx_conv1_2 = mx.sym.Convolution(data=ctx_relu1_1, kernel=(3, 3), pad=(1, 1), num_filter=num_class, name='ctx_conv1_2', workspace=2048)
    ctx_relu1_2 = mx.sym.Activation(data=ctx_conv1_2, act_type='relu', name='ctx_relu1_2')

    ctx_conv2_1 = mx.sym.Convolution(data=ctx_relu1_2, kernel=(3, 3), pad=(2, 2), num_filter=num_class, dilate=(2, 2), name='ctx_conv2_1', workspace=2048)
    ctx_relu2_1 = mx.sym.Activation(data=ctx_conv2_1, act_type='relu', name='ctx_relu2_1')

    ctx_conv3_1 = mx.sym.Convolution(data=ctx_relu2_1, kernel=(3, 3), pad=(4, 4), num_filter=num_class, dilate=(4, 4), name='ctx_conv3_1', workspace=2048)
    ctx_relu3_1 = mx.sym.Activation(data=ctx_conv3_1, act_type='relu', name='ctx_relu3_1')

    ctx_conv4_1 = mx.sym.Convolution(data=ctx_relu3_1, kernel=(3, 3), pad=(8, 8), num_filter=num_class, dilate=(8, 8), name='ctx_conv4_1', workspace=2048)
    ctx_relu4_1 = mx.sym.Activation(data=ctx_conv4_1, act_type='relu', name='ctx_relu4_1')

    ctx_conv5_1 = mx.sym.Convolution(data=ctx_relu4_1, kernel=(3, 3), pad=(16, 16), num_filter=num_class, dilate=(16, 16), name='ctx_conv5_1', workspace=2048)
    ctx_relu5_1 = mx.sym.Activation(data=ctx_conv5_1, act_type='relu', name='ctx_relu5_1')

    ctx_conv6_1 = mx.sym.Convolution(data=ctx_relu5_1, kernel=(3, 3), pad=(32, 32), num_filter=num_class, dilate=(32, 32), name='ctx_conv6_1', workspace=2048)
    ctx_relu6_1 = mx.sym.Activation(data=ctx_conv6_1, act_type='relu', name='ctx_relu6_1')

    ctx_conv7_1 = mx.sym.Convolution(data=ctx_relu6_1, kernel=(3, 3), pad=(64, 64), num_filter=num_class, dilate=(64, 64), name='ctx_conv7_1', workspace=2048)
    ctx_relu7_1 = mx.sym.Activation(data=ctx_conv7_1, act_type='relu', name='ctx_relu7_1')

    ctx_fc1 = mx.sym.Convolution(data=ctx_relu7_1, kernel=(3, 3), pad=(1, 1), num_filter=num_class, name='ctx_fc1', workspace=2048)
    ctx_fc1_relu1 = mx.sym.Activation(data=ctx_fc1, act_type='relu', name='ctx_fc1_relu1')

    ctx_final = mx.sym.Convolution(data=ctx_fc1_relu1, kernel=(1, 1), num_filter=num_class, name='ctx_final', workspace=2048)
    softmax = mx.sym.SoftmaxOutput(data=ctx_final, multi_output=True, use_ignore=True,  ignore_label=255, name="softmax")  

i use memonger to compute the memory required for one image as input:

    cost = memonger.get_cost(softmax, data=(1, 3, 1396, 1396))

the required memory is 6954MB, which is much less than 12GB. but when i use 2 images as input and 2 Titan X gpu, "cudaMalloc failed" occurred, i don't understand why the memory is not enough. 

i use mx.model.FeedForward to train the network, thanks

issue_title: _set_attr() needs to be public (or list_attr needs to be private)
issue_body:
Right now _set_attr() is a private function. But list_attr() is public. So basically if the user can't set the attributes, then list_attr() will always return `{}`. 

They should either both be public or both be private.

(in symbol.py)
